title: How Long Does Buying a Home in the UK Really Take?
excerpt: The complete week-by-week guide to UK property buying timelines - what happens
column8:
If you've been told buying a home in the UK takes "about three months", that's broadly right - but it tells you almost nothing useful. This guide breaks every stage down into what's happening, who's doing it, and what you can do to keep things moving.

Before you start

The fastest completions share one thing in common - the buyer had their finances ready before they made an offer. That means an Agreement in Principle, a deposit sitting ready, and a solicitor on standby.

title: Off-Market Property: What It Is and How to Find It
excerpt: How to access properties that never appear on Rightmove - and why buyers who win off-market deals use a buyer's agent.
content:
Around 25-30% of UK property transactions happen before the home ever reaches Rightmove. These off-market deals go to buyers with the right connections.

Why sellers go off-market

Privacy, avoiding the disruption of viewings, or simply trusting an agent to bring a qualified buyer directly. Probate sales, corporate relocations, and downsizing retirees are the most common sources.

title: Stamp Duty 2025: The Complete Guide for Buyers
excerpt: Everything buyers need to know about stamp duty in 2025 - rates thresholds first-time buyer relief and the additional property surcharge.
content:
Stamp Duty Land Tax (SDLT) is one of the largest costs of buying a property in England. The thresholds differ depending on whether you're a first-time buyer, moving home, or purchasing an additional property.

Current stamp duty rates (2025)

For your main home: 0% on the first £250,000; 5% on £250,001-£925,000; 10% on £925,001-£1.5M; 12% above £1.5M.

title: UK Property Market: May 2025 Update
excerpt: Average asking prices rose 2.3% in Q1 2025. Here's what it means for buyers and how to navigate a competitive market.
content:
The latest data from HM Land Registry shows average UK house prices increased by 2.3% in the first quarter of 2025, driven by continued demand pressure in London, Manchester, and Bristol.

What this means for buyers

Competition for well-priced stock remains intense, particularly for family homes under £600,000.
